title: Cat Seizures Lasting Over One Minute: Immediate Steps & Veterinary Triage
meta_desc: If your cat has a seizure lasting more than one minute, it is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn what to do and how to help your pet.
content_html:
What Is a Cat Seizure?

A seizure is a sudden, involuntary disturbance in your cat's brain activity. It often manifests as uncontrollable muscle contractions, paddling of the limbs, loss of bladder control, or a total loss of consciousness. It is a frightening experience for any pet owner, but understanding that your cat is not conscious or feeling pain during the event can help you stay calm and provide the necessary care.

Is It an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level is High. While any first-time seizure requires a veterinary consultation, a seizure lasting more than one minute is a critical medical emergency known as status epilepticus. Prolonged seizures can cause a dangerous spike in body temperature (hyperthermia) and may lead to permanent brain damage or organ failure. If the seizure persists for more than 60 seconds, or if your cat has multiple seizures in a row, you must transport them to an emergency clinic immediately.

Why a Video or Photo Helps Triage

If you are able to do so safely, recording a short video of the seizure can be an invaluable tool for your veterinarian. Many neurological or cardiac events can mimic seizures. By seeing the specific movements, the cat's level of awareness, and the duration of the event, the veterinary team can more accurately diagnose the cause and begin the correct life-saving treatment the moment you arrive at the hospital.

faqs.question: What should I do while my cat is having a seizure?
faqs.answer: Clear the area of sharp objects or furniture to prevent injury. Do not put your hands or anything else in your cat's mouth, as they may bite involuntarily. Keep the room dark and quiet to reduce stimulation.

faqs.question: Will my cat be okay after the seizure ends?
faqs.answer: After a seizure, cats enter a 'post-ictal' phase where they may be disoriented, temporarily blind, or restless for minutes to hours. Even if they seem to recover, a seizure lasting over a minute requires immediate professional evaluation.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human medication to stop the seizure?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human medications or extra doses of existing pet medications without direct instruction from a veterinarian, as this can be toxic or worsen the condition.
